title: TwoDisjointNode
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W_1, W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of the output nodes from $G$. Then the CADMG obtained from first node-splitting on $W_1$ and then node-splitting on $W_2$ is the same CADMG that arises from first node-splitting on $W_2$ and then node-splitting on $W_1$: \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]

\phantomsection\label{claim_3_10}

% NOTE on the iterated-splitting carrier mismatch (Lean encoding).
%
% In a Lean encoding that follows def_3_12 literally and realises the
% tagged copies W^o, W^i as type-level disjoint sums, the carriers of
% (G_{\swig(W_1)})_{\swig(W_2)} and G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} are formally
% distinct types (twice-tagged vs. once-tagged on J \cup V), even though
% the LN identifies them set-theoretically via def_3_12's unsplit-
% injection convention "v^o := v^i := v for v \in J \cup (V \sm W)". The
% one-sentence clarification inside the lemma body below ("=" is read up
% to the canonical bijection of carriers) is the load-bearing reading;
% the concrete choice of how to render this identification in Lean
% (CDMG-transport along a node-bijection, CDMG-isomorphism structure,
% or some other equivalence layer) is deferred to the Lean-formalization
% worker, which is expected to reuse the `flattenSplit` map already
% introduced in claim_3_7's solved file (TwoDisjointNode.lean) -- the
% same constructor algebra applies here because def_3_12 reuses
% def_3_11's tagged-copy convention. See workspace_claim_3_10.md for
% the encoding-design discussion.

\begin{Lem}[Two disjoint node-splitting hard interventions commute]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg} (namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \ins V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$)), and rely on the node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG) construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. Let
    \[ W_1 \ins V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \ins V \]
    be two subsets of the output-node set of $G$, subject to the disjointness side condition
    \[ W_1 \cap W_2 \;=\; \emptyset. \]
    All three quantifiers ``for every CDMG $G$'', ``for every $W_1 \ins V$'', and ``for every $W_2 \ins V$ with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$'' are read \emph{universally}; in particular the (vacuously disjoint) case $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$ is admitted.

    \emph{Reading of ``CDMG'' versus ``CADMG'' in the LN's prose.}
    The LN's literal text opens with ``Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG'' and concludes ``the CADMG\ldots is the same CADMG\ldots'', referring to all three of the iterated SWIGs in the displayed equality as CADMGs. We read this entire row as a statement about \emph{CDMGs} throughout, in line with how def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} is implemented at the Lean type level (its constructor takes a CDMG to a CDMG with no acyclicity attribute checked internally), and in parallel with the analogous reading made in \ref{claim_3_7} for the regular node-splitting case. The bridge between this CDMG-level reading and the LN's literal CADMG-level reading is supplied by \ref{claim_3_9} (\emph{SwigAcyclic}): under the standing CADMG hypothesis on $G$ (which adds the acyclicity hypothesis of def \ref{def-acylic} on top of the CDMG typing of def \ref{def-cdmg}), \ref{claim_3_9} establishes that $G_{\swig(W)}$ is itself acyclic, hence a CADMG; iterating this twice, both $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$ are CADMGs, and so is the right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$. The asserted equality below is consequently first an equality of CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}) at the type level; the upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is recovered by transporting the CADMG-witnesses of \ref{claim_3_9} along this CDMG-equality (acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG, so the upgrade is automatic once both sides are known to be acyclic CDMGs).

    \emph{Recap of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s carrier shape (set-theoretic).}
    For a CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and $W \ins V$, def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} defines $G_{\swig(W)} = (J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)}, L_{\swig(W)})$ by
    \begin{align*}
        J_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; J \dcup W^i, \\
        V_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; (V \sm W) \dcup W^o, \\
        E_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC, \\
        L_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC,
    \end{align*}
    where $W^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W \rC$ and $W^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W \rC$ are two type-level-tagged copies of $W$ (formally distinct from each other and from every element of $J \cup V$), and the LN's shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ governs how the source/target labels in the set-builders for $E_{\swig(W)}$ and $L_{\swig(W)}$ resolve when the underlying node lies outside $W$. In particular the input-side tags $W^i$ are placed into the input-node set $J_{\swig(W)}$ and \emph{not} into the output-node set $V_{\swig(W)}$: this is the key difference from regular node-splitting (def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, item~ii.), whose output-node set carries \emph{both} $W^0$ and $W^1$ copies. In the Lean encoding underlying the project's formalisation, the tagged copies $W^o$, $W^i$ and the untagged $V \sm W$ piece are realised as the three constructors \texttt{SplitNode.copy0}, \texttt{SplitNode.copy1}, \texttt{SplitNode.unsplit} of the same \texttt{SplitNode} type used for def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} (def_3_11), reusing its constructor algebra; here the LN's superscripts $^o$ and $^i$ collapse onto \texttt{SplitNode.copy0} and \texttt{SplitNode.copy1} respectively, and $J \cup (V \sm W)$ collapses onto \texttt{SplitNode.unsplit} via the LN's shorthand convention. (Identifying $^o$ with \texttt{copy0} and $^i$ with \texttt{copy1} matches the convention used in def_3_11/claim_3_7 in this subsection.)

    \emph{Well-typedness of the iterated SWIG.}
    The inner two terms of the displayed equality below are iterated SWIGs of the form $\lp G_{\swig(W_i)} \rp_{\swig(W_j)}$ (with $\{i, j\} = \{1, 2\}$), in which $\swig(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\swig(W_i)}$ produced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. For this outer application to be well-typed per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s precondition ``$W \ins V$ of the input CDMG'', we need $W_j$ to be (a subset of) the output-node set of $G_{\swig(W_i)}$, i.e.\
    \[ W_j \;\ins\; V_{\swig(W_i)} \;=\; (V \sm W_i) \;\dcup\; W_i^o, \]
    where the equality of carriers is item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} and $W_i^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W_i \rC$ is the output-side tagged copy of $W_i$ introduced there (note that, in contrast to regular node-splitting, the input-side copy $W_i^i$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_i)}$ rather than $V_{\swig(W_i)}$, so the iterated output-node set carries only the single tagged copy $W_i^o$). By the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, every $w \in W_j$ satisfies $w \in V$ (since $W_j \ins V$) and $w \notin W_i$ (by disjointness), so $w \in V \sm W_i$. Through def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s notational shorthand ``$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_i)$'', the set $V \sm W_i$ is realised as the untagged piece of the disjoint union $V_{\swig(W_i)} = (V \sm W_i) \dcup W_i^o$, i.e.\ the inclusion
    \[ V \sm W_i \;\hookrightarrow\; V_{\swig(W_i)} \]
    sends $v \mapsto v$ unchanged. Hence
    \[ W_j \;\ins\; V \sm W_i \;\ins\; V_{\swig(W_i)}, \]
    so $\lp G_{\swig(W_i)} \rp_{\swig(W_j)}$ is defined per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. The analogous spell-out for the swapped pair (with $i$ and $j$ exchanged) is the same argument with the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ exchanged, using the same disjointness condition $W_1 \cap W_2 = W_2 \cap W_1 = \emptyset$. The right-hand-side single SWIG $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ is well-typed per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} because $W_1 \dcup W_2 \ins V$: by $W_1 \ins V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ together with disjointness, the union $W_1 \cup W_2$ is itself a disjoint union $W_1 \dcup W_2$ and remains a subset of $V$.

    \emph{The conclusion.}
    The CDMGs $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$, $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$, and $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality
    \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \]
    is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:
    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
    \end{enumerate}
    Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's ``symmetry under swap'' reading
    \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \]
    by transitivity of equality on the shared right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

    The equalities in (a) and (b) are equalities of CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}) read \emph{up to the canonical bijection} of the iterated and single-SWIG node-set carriers induced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s unsplit-injection convention ``$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$'' (the iterated carriers $J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$, $V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$ and the single-step carriers $J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, $V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ are not literally the same sets as produced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}, but the corresponding pairs are canonically identified by matching corresponding tagged copies: under the Lean \texttt{SplitNode} encoding this identification is exactly the \texttt{flattenSplit} map introduced in \ref{claim_3_7}, which collapses \texttt{SplitNode (SplitNode Node)} onto \texttt{SplitNode Node} by sending nested \texttt{copy0}/\texttt{copy1}/\texttt{unsplit} constructors to the appropriate single-level constructor, and the present row's Lean file is expected to import that map verbatim from claim_3_7's solved file).
\end{Lem}

\end{document}

\begin{Lem}[Two disjoint node-splitting hard interventions commute]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg} (namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, and $L$ consists of unordered pairs of distinct vertices in $V$), and rely on the node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG) construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. Let
    \[ W_1 \ins V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \ins V \]
    be two subsets of the output-node set of $G$, subject to the disjointness side condition
    \[ W_1 \cap W_2 \;=\; \emptyset. \]
    All three quantifiers ``for every CDMG $G$'', ``for every $W_1 \ins V$'', and ``for every $W_2 \ins V$ with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$'' are read \emph{universally}; in particular the (vacuously disjoint) case $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$ is admitted.

    \emph{Reading of ``CDMG'' versus ``CADMG'' in the LN's prose.}
    The LN's literal text opens with ``Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG'' and concludes ``the CADMG\ldots is the same CADMG\ldots'', referring to all three of the iterated SWIGs in the displayed equality as CADMGs. We read this entire row as a statement about \emph{CDMGs} throughout, in line with how def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} is implemented at the Lean type level (its constructor takes a CDMG to a CDMG with no acyclicity attribute checked internally), and in parallel with the analogous reading made in \ref{claim_3_7} for the regular node-splitting case. The bridge between this CDMG-level reading and the LN's literal CADMG-level reading is supplied by \ref{claim_3_9} (\emph{SwigAcyclic}): under the standing CADMG hypothesis on $G$ (which adds the acyclicity hypothesis of def \ref{def-acylic} on top of the CDMG typing of def \ref{def-cdmg}), \ref{claim_3_9} establishes that $G_{\swig(W)}$ is itself acyclic, hence a CADMG; iterating this twice, both $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$ are CADMGs, and so is the right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$. The asserted equality below is consequently first an equality of CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}) at the type level; the upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is recovered by transporting the CADMG-witnesses of \ref{claim_3_9} along this CDMG-equality (acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG, so the upgrade is automatic once both sides are known to be acyclic CDMGs).

    \emph{Recap of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s carrier shape (set-theoretic).}
    For a CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and $W \ins V$, def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} defines $G_{\swig(W)} = (J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)}, L_{\swig(W)})$ by
    \begin{align*}
        J_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; J \dcup W^i, \\
        V_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; (V \sm W) \dcup W^o, \\
        E_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC, \\
        L_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC,
    \end{align*}
    where $W^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W \rC$ and $W^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W \rC$ are two type-level-tagged copies of $W$ (formally distinct from each other and from every element of $J \cup V$), and the LN's shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ governs how the source/target labels in the set-builders for $E_{\swig(W)}$ and $L_{\swig(W)}$ resolve when the underlying node lies outside $W$. In particular the input-side tags $W^i$ are placed into the input-node set $J_{\swig(W)}$ and \emph{not} into the output-node set $V_{\swig(W)}$: this is the key difference from regular node-splitting (def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, item~ii.), whose output-node set carries \emph{both} $W^0$ and $W^1$ copies. In the Lean encoding underlying the project's formalisation, the tagged copies $W^o$, $W^i$ and the untagged $V \sm W$ piece are realised as the three constructors \texttt{SplitNode.copy0}, \texttt{SplitNode.copy1}, \texttt{SplitNode.unsplit} of the same \texttt{SplitNode} type used for def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} (def_3_11), reusing its constructor algebra; here the LN's superscripts $^o$ and $^i$ collapse onto \texttt{SplitNode.copy0} and \texttt{SplitNode.copy1} respectively, and $J \cup (V \sm W)$ collapses onto \texttt{SplitNode.unsplit} via the LN's shorthand convention. (Identifying $^o$ with \texttt{copy0} and $^i$ with \texttt{copy1} matches the convention used in def_3_11/claim_3_7 in this subsection.)

    \emph{Well-typedness of the iterated SWIG.}
    The inner two terms of the displayed equality below are iterated SWIGs of the form $\lp G_{\swig(W_i)} \rp_{\swig(W_j)}$ (with $\{i, j\} = \{1, 2\}$), in which $\swig(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\swig(W_i)}$ produced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. For this outer application to be well-typed per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s precondition ``$W \ins V$ of the input CDMG'', we need $W_j$ to be (a subset of) the output-node set of $G_{\swig(W_i)}$, i.e.\
    \[ W_j \;\ins\; V_{\swig(W_i)} \;=\; (V \sm W_i) \;\dcup\; W_i^o, \]
    where the equality of carriers is item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} and $W_i^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W_i \rC$ is the output-side tagged copy of $W_i$ introduced there (note that, in contrast to regular node-splitting, the input-side copy $W_i^i$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_i)}$ rather than $V_{\swig(W_i)}$, so the iterated output-node set carries only the single tagged copy $W_i^o$). By the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, every $w \in W_j$ satisfies $w \in V$ (since $W_j \ins V$) and $w \notin W_i$ (by disjointness), so $w \in V \sm W_i$. Through def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s notational shorthand ``$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_i)$'', the set $V \sm W_i$ is realised as the untagged piece of the disjoint union $V_{\swig(W_i)} = (V \sm W_i) \dcup W_i^o$, i.e.\ the inclusion
    \[ V \sm W_i \;\hookrightarrow\; V_{\swig(W_i)} \]
    sends $v \mapsto v$ unchanged. Hence
    \[ W_j \;\ins\; V \sm W_i \;\ins\; V_{\swig(W_i)}, \]
    so $\lp G_{\swig(W_i)} \rp_{\swig(W_j)}$ is defined per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}. The analogous spell-out for the swapped pair (with $i$ and $j$ exchanged) is the same argument with the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ exchanged, using the same disjointness condition $W_1 \cap W_2 = W_2 \cap W_1 = \emptyset$. The right-hand-side single SWIG $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ is well-typed per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} because $W_1 \dcup W_2 \ins V$: by $W_1 \ins V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ together with disjointness, the union $W_1 \cup W_2$ is itself a disjoint union $W_1 \dcup W_2$ and remains a subset of $V$.

    \emph{The conclusion.}
    The CDMGs $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$, $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$, and $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality
    \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \]
    is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:
    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
    \end{enumerate}
    Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's ``symmetry under swap'' reading
    \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \]
    by transitivity of equality on the shared right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

    The equalities in (a) and (b) are equalities of CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}) read \emph{up to the canonical bijection} of the iterated and single-SWIG node-set carriers induced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s unsplit-injection convention ``$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$'' (the iterated carriers $J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$, $V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$ and the single-step carriers $J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, $V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ are not literally the same sets as produced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}, but the corresponding pairs are canonically identified by matching corresponding tagged copies: under the Lean \texttt{SplitNode} encoding this identification is exactly the \texttt{flattenSplit} map introduced in \ref{claim_3_7}, which collapses \texttt{SplitNode (SplitNode Node)} onto \texttt{SplitNode Node} by sending nested \texttt{copy0}/\texttt{copy1}/\texttt{unsplit} constructors to the appropriate single-level constructor, and the present row's Lean file is expected to import that map verbatim from claim_3_7's solved file).
\end{Lem}

%      spells it out in full, so the proof just refers back),
% (iii) the LN's "Node sets" paragraph collapses J and V together; we

%       ``Output nodes'' to match the componentwise-on-(J,V,E,L) structure
%       of the proof,
% (iv) every ``similarly'' / ``same case analysis'' in the LN is expanded
%      into explicit case enumeration (no ellipses), and the case
%      ``$v \notin W_1 \cup W_2$'' is split into its $J$-vs-$V$ subcases
%      where the relevant typing differs,

%      closing with a transitivity-of-equality remark recovering the LN's
%      triple equality through the shared right-hand side,
% (vi) a closing ``Carrier-bijection convention'' paragraph acknowledges
%      the read-up-to-canonical-bijection convention noted at the bottom
%      of the statement block.
\begin{proof}
We prove sub-claim (a), $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, by checking the equality of the two CDMGs componentwise on each of the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} in turn; sub-claim (b), $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, is then obtained by an explicit symmetry argument at the end. Both sub-claims rely on the well-typedness check already established in the statement block above to license the outer $\swig(\cdot)$ application: in particular, by the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, every $w \in W_2$ satisfies $w \in V \sm W_1 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ (and symmetrically every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in V \sm W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_2)}$).

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Notation for SWIG copies.}
For $k \in \{1, 2\}$ and $w \in W_k$, write $w^{o_k}$ and $w^{i_k}$ for the two tagged copies produced by $\swig(W_k)$ per def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}; the subscript $k$ records which of the two SWIG steps introduced the tag. The output-side copy $w^{o_k}$ sits in $V_{\swig(W_k)}$ while the input-side copy $w^{i_k}$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_k)}$ (items~i.\ and~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}), in contrast to regular node-splitting (def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}) where both tagged copies sit in the output-node set. By def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}'s unsplit-injection shorthand, $v^{o_k} := v^{i_k} := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_k)$. For an element $v \in J \cup V$ we will also write $v^i$ and $v^o$ for the single-step tagging used on $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $v^i := v^{i_1}$ and $v^o := v^{o_1}$ if $v \in W_1$,
    \item $v^i := v^{i_2}$ and $v^o := v^{o_2}$ if $v \in W_2$,
    \item $v^i := v^o := v$ if $v \in J \cup (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2))$
\end{itemize}
(the three cases are exhaustive and pairwise disjoint by disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$; the $o$-version is only used for $v \in V$ since $L \ins V \times V$ and the output-side tagging is never applied to inputs $v \in J$ in $E$ or $L$). This is the canonical bijection of carriers spelled out at the bottom of the statement block.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Proof of (a): composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$.}
We verify the equality $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ on each of the four components.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Input nodes ($J$-component).}
By item~i.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the inner SWIG,
\[ J_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^{i_1}. \]
Applying item~i.\ again to the outer SWIG (acting on the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, with split set $W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ as licensed by the well-typedness check),
\[
    J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{\swig(W_1)} \;\dcup\; W_2^{i_2} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^{i_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{i_2}.
\]
On the other hand, item~i.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the single-step SWIG on $G$ with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2$ gives
\[ J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; (W_1 \dcup W_2)^i, \]
and the tag-by-tag identification of the single-step input-side copies $(W_1 \dcup W_2)^i = W_1^{i_1} \dcup W_2^{i_2}$ (per the canonical bijection of carriers in the notation block above) yields
\[ J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^{i_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{i_2}. \]
The two input-node sets match. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Output nodes ($V$-component).}
By item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the outer SWIG (acting on the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, with split set $W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$),
\[ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lp V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^{o_2}. \]
Item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the inner SWIG gives $V_{\swig(W_1)} = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1}$. By disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, the elements of $W_2 \ins V$ all lie in $V \sm W_1$, and (via the unsplit-injection identification $V \sm W_1 \hookrightarrow V_{\swig(W_1)}$) they embed into the untagged piece of the above disjoint union, disjointly from $W_1^{o_1}$. Removing $W_2$ from $V_{\swig(W_1)}$ thus only affects the untagged piece, giving
\[ V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2 \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_1^{o_1}. \]
Substituting,
\[ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_1^{o_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{o_2}. \]
On the other hand, item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the single-step SWIG on $G$ with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2$ gives
\[ V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)) \;\dcup\; (W_1 \dcup W_2)^o, \]
and $V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2) = V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$ together with the tag-by-tag identification $(W_1 \dcup W_2)^o = W_1^{o_1} \dcup W_2^{o_2}$ (per the canonical bijection of carriers) yields
\[ V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_1^{o_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{o_2}. \]
The two output-node sets match. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Directed edges ($E$-component).}
By item~iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the inner SWIG,
\[ E_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; \lC v_1^{i_1} \tuh v_2^{o_1} \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC. \]
(Note that this is a single set-comprehension; there is no separate transfer-edge contribution as there is for regular node-splitting in def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, since item~iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} omits the ``$\dcup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \st w \in W \rC$'' clause -- the SWIG construction does \emph{not} introduce inner transfer arcs from $W^o$ back to $W^i$.) Applying the outer $\swig(W_2)$ to the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, item~iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} gives
\[
    E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1')^{i_2} \tuh (v_2')^{o_2} \st v_1' \tuh v_2' \in E_{\swig(W_1)} \rC.
\]
We trace each generator $v_1^{i_1} \tuh v_2^{o_1}$ of $E_{\swig(W_1)}$ (arising from $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E$, so $v_1 \in J \cup V$ and $v_2 \in V$ by def \ref{def-cdmg}) through the outer tagging, case-splitting on each endpoint independently.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Head endpoint $v_1^{i_1}$.} The three cases are exhaustive and pairwise disjoint by $J \cap V = \emptyset$ together with the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Case $v_1 \in W_1$.} Then $v_1^{i_1} \in W_1^{i_1}$ by the inner-SWIG tagged copy. By item~i.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the inner SWIG, $W_1^{i_1} \ins J_{\swig(W_1)}$, hence by def \ref{def-cdmg}'s axiom $J_{\swig(W_1)} \cap V_{\swig(W_1)} = \emptyset$ on the inner CDMG, the tagged piece $W_1^{i_1}$ is disjoint from the outer SWIG's input subset $W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$; in particular $v_1^{i_1} \notin W_2$. The outer unsplit-injection $u^{i_2} := u$ for $u \in J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup (V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2)$ then gives $(v_1^{i_1})^{i_2} = v_1^{i_1} = v_1^i$ (in the $v^i$ notation of the notation block, using the case ``$v_1 \in W_1$'').
    \item \emph{Case $v_1 \in W_2$.} Then $v_1 \in V$ (since $W_2 \ins V$), and by disjointness $v_1 \notin W_1$, so the inner unsplit-injection gives $v_1^{i_1} = v_1$. The outer SWIG then maps $v_1 \mapsto v_1^{i_2} = v_1^i$ (using the case ``$v_1 \in W_2$'' of the notation block).
    \item \emph{Case $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2))$.} Then $v_1 \notin W_1$ and $v_1 \notin W_2$, so $v_1^{i_1} = v_1$ (inner unsplit). The outer SWIG then fixes $v_1$: since $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)) \ins J \cup (V \sm W_2) \ins J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup (V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2)$ (the second inclusion uses the inner SWIG's $J \dcup W_1^{i_1} = J_{\swig(W_1)}$ and $(V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1} = V_{\swig(W_1)}$, into which $J$ embeds and $V \sm W_1 \sm W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$ embeds via the unsplit-injection), we have $(v_1)^{i_2} = v_1 = v_1^i$ (using the third case of the notation block).
\end{itemize}

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Tail endpoint $v_2^{o_1}$.} The same three-case analysis, with $o_k$ in place of $i_k$ throughout and the slightly tighter typing $v_2 \in V$ (so the third case reads ``$v_2 \in V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)$''):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Case $v_2 \in W_1$.} Then $v_2^{o_1} \in W_1^{o_1}$ by the inner-SWIG tagged copy. By item~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}, $W_1^{o_1} \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ and is disjoint from the untagged piece $V \sm W_1 \supseteq W_2$ in the disjoint union $V_{\swig(W_1)} = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1}$; hence $v_2^{o_1} \notin W_2$. The outer unsplit-injection then gives $(v_2^{o_1})^{o_2} = v_2^{o_1} = v_2^o$ (using the case ``$v_2 \in W_1$'' of the notation block).
    \item \emph{Case $v_2 \in W_2$.} Then by disjointness $v_2 \notin W_1$, so the inner unsplit-injection gives $v_2^{o_1} = v_2$. The outer SWIG then maps $v_2 \mapsto v_2^{o_2} = v_2^o$ (using the case ``$v_2 \in W_2$'' of the notation block).
    \item \emph{Case $v_2 \in V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)$.} Then $v_2 \notin W_1$ and $v_2 \notin W_2$, so $v_2^{o_1} = v_2$ (inner unsplit) and $v_2 \in V \sm W_1 \sm W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2$, so $(v_2)^{o_2} = v_2 = v_2^o$ (outer unsplit, using the third case of the notation block).
\end{itemize}

Hence the image of $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E$ under the iterated tagging is exactly $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o$, and the collection of such images is
\[ \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC. \]
On the other hand, item~iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the single-step SWIG with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2 \ins V$ yields directly
\[ E_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \]
under the same $v^i$/$v^o$ tagging conventions. Hence
\[ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; E_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
\checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Bidirected edges ($L$-component).}
By item~iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the inner SWIG,
\[ L_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; \lC v_1^{o_1} \huh v_2^{o_1} \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \]
Applying the outer $\swig(W_2)$, item~iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} gives
\[
    L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1')^{o_2} \huh (v_2')^{o_2} \st v_1' \huh v_2' \in L_{\swig(W_1)} \rC.
\]
For a generator $v_1^{o_1} \huh v_2^{o_1}$ (arising from $v_1 \huh v_2 \in L$, with $v_1, v_2 \in V$ by def \ref{def-cdmg}'s typing $L \ins V \times V$), the same three-case analysis as for the tail endpoint of the directed-edge case applies to each $o$-superscripted endpoint independently:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Case $v \in W_1$.} Then $v^{o_1} \in W_1^{o_1} \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ and (by the inner-SWIG carrier identity $V_{\swig(W_1)} = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1}$ together with disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ giving $W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$) $v^{o_1} \notin W_2$; the outer unsplit-injection fixes it: $(v^{o_1})^{o_2} = v^{o_1} = v^o$.
    \item \emph{Case $v \in W_2$.} Then by disjointness $v \notin W_1$, so the inner unsplit-injection gives $v^{o_1} = v$; the outer SWIG then maps $v \mapsto v^{o_2} = v^o$.
    \item \emph{Case $v \in V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)$.} Then $v \notin W_1$ and $v \notin W_2$, so $v^{o_1} = v$ (inner unsplit) and $(v)^{o_2} = v = v^o$ (outer unsplit). (The fourth a priori case ``$v \in J$'' is excluded by def \ref{def-cdmg}'s constraint $L \ins V \times V$, which forces both endpoints of any $L$-generator to lie in $V$; so the three cases above are exhaustive on the endpoints actually appearing in the set-builder for $L$.)
\end{itemize}
Hence
\[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \]
On the other hand, item~iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} applied to the single-step SWIG with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2$ yields exactly the same set under the same $v^o$ tagging:
\[ L_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \]
Hence
\[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; L_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
\checkmark

\smallskip
Combining the four componentwise equalities above, the CDMGs $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ agree field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}). This establishes sub-claim (a).

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Proof of (b): composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$.}
Sub-claim (b) is obtained by swapping the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the argument for (a). The four-component verification above depends on the pair of subsets only through their symbolic roles: the input-node identity and the output-node identity follow from items~i.\ and~ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}; the directed-edge and bidirected-edge identities follow from items~iii.\ and~iv., respectively, together with the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, which is symmetric in $(W_1, W_2)$ ($W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset \Leftrightarrow W_2 \cap W_1 = \emptyset$). The well-typedness side-condition needed to license the outer $\swig(\cdot)$ application also has a symmetric counterpart, established in the statement block: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in V \sm W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_2)}$. Re-running the four-component check above with the substitution $W_1 \leftrightarrow W_2$ throughout therefore yields
\[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_2 \dcup W_1)}. \]
This is precisely sub-claim (a) instantiated at the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$. We emphasise that sub-claim (b) is \emph{not} a literal instance of (a): the iterated SWIG on the left-hand side of (b) is built from the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$, and the right-hand-side disjoint union appearing in (a) for that swapped pair is $W_2 \dcup W_1$, not the $W_1 \dcup W_2$ in the statement of (b). The two right-hand sides agree by commutativity of disjoint union, $W_2 \dcup W_1 = W_1 \dcup W_2$, which gives
\[ G_{\swig(W_2 \dcup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \]
as CDMGs: def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention} depends on $W$ only through the underlying set $W \ins V$ (each of items~i.--iv.\ refers to $W$ only via the operations $V \sm W$, $W^o$, $W^i$, and the membership predicate ``$\cdot \in W$''), and equal sets produce equal CDMGs componentwise. Chaining the two equalities,
\[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_2 \dcup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}, \]
establishes sub-claim (b).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Recovery of the LN's triple equality.}
By sub-claims (a) and (b) -- both of which equate their respective iterated SWIG with the single right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ -- transitivity of equality on that shared right-hand side yields
\[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}, \]
which is the LN's stated triple equality and in particular recovers the swap-symmetry reading $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Carrier-bijection convention.}
The equalities above are read up to the canonical bijection of iterated and single-step SWIG node-set carriers spelled out at the bottom of the statement block: the iterated carriers $J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$, $V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$ and their single-step counterparts $J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, $V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ are not literally the same sets as produced by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}, but the corresponding pairs are canonically identified by matching tagged copies. Under the Lean \texttt{SplitNode} encoding, this identification is exactly the \texttt{flattenSplit} map introduced in \ref{claim_3_7} (which collapses \texttt{SplitNode (SplitNode Node)} onto \texttt{SplitNode Node} by sending nested \texttt{copy0}/\texttt{copy1}/\texttt{unsplit} constructors to the appropriate single-level constructor, and is imported verbatim by the present row's Lean file); the equalities ``$=$'' in sub-claims (a) and (b) above and in the LN's displayed triple equality are read along that map.
\end{proof}

\end{document}

Lemma — Two disjoint node-splitting hard interventions commute

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg (namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, and $L$ consists of unordered pairs of distinct vertices in $V$), and rely on the node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG) construction of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention. Let \[ W_1 \ins V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \ins V \] be two subsets of the output-node set of $G$, subject to the disjointness side condition \[ W_1 \cap W_2 \;=\; \emptyset. \] All three quantifiers “for every CDMG $G$”, “for every $W_1 \ins V$”, and “for every $W_2 \ins V$ with $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$” are read universally; in particular the (vacuously disjoint) case $W_1 = W_2 = \emptyset$ is admitted.

Reading of “CDMG” versus “CADMG” in the LN's prose. The LN's literal text opens with “Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG” and concludes “the CADMG… is the same CADMG…”, referring to all three of the iterated SWIGs in the displayed equality as CADMGs. We read this entire row as a statement about CDMGs throughout, in line with how def def:G_node-splitting_intervention is implemented at the Lean type level (its constructor takes a CDMG to a CDMG with no acyclicity attribute checked internally), and in parallel with the analogous reading made in claim_3_7 for the regular node-splitting case. The bridge between this CDMG-level reading and the LN's literal CADMG-level reading is supplied by claim_3_9 (SwigAcyclic): under the standing CADMG hypothesis on $G$ (which adds the acyclicity hypothesis of def def-acylic on top of the CDMG typing of def def-cdmg), claim_3_9 establishes that $G_{\swig(W)}$ is itself acyclic, hence a CADMG; iterating this twice, both $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$ are CADMGs, and so is the right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$. The asserted equality below is consequently first an equality of CDMGs (def def-cdmg) at the type level; the upgrade to an equality of CADMGs is recovered by transporting the CADMG-witnesses of claim_3_9 along this CDMG-equality (acyclicity is a propositional condition on a fixed CDMG, so the upgrade is automatic once both sides are known to be acyclic CDMGs).

Recap of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's carrier shape (set-theoretic). For a CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and $W \ins V$, def def:G_node-splitting_intervention defines $G_{\swig(W)} = (J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)}, L_{\swig(W)})$ by \[\begin{aligned} J_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; J \dcup W^i, \\ V_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; (V \sm W) \dcup W^o, \\ E_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; \lC (v_1^i, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC, \\ L_{\swig(W)} \;&:=\; \lC (v_1^o, v_2^o) \st (v_1, v_2) \in L \rC, \end{aligned}\] where $W^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W \rC$ and $W^i := \lC w^i \st w \in W \rC$ are two type-level-tagged copies of $W$ (formally distinct from each other and from every element of $J \cup V$), and the LN's shorthand $v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ governs how the source/target labels in the set-builders for $E_{\swig(W)}$ and $L_{\swig(W)}$ resolve when the underlying node lies outside $W$. In particular the input-side tags $W^i$ are placed into the input-node set $J_{\swig(W)}$ and not into the output-node set $V_{\swig(W)}$: this is the key difference from regular node-splitting (def def:G_node-splitting, item ii.), whose output-node set carries both $W^0$ and $W^1$ copies. In the Lean encoding underlying the project's formalisation, the tagged copies $W^o$, $W^i$ and the untagged $V \sm W$ piece are realised as the three constructors SplitNode.copy0, SplitNode.copy1, SplitNode.unsplit of the same SplitNode type used for def def:G_node-splitting (def_3_11), reusing its constructor algebra; here the LN's superscripts $^o$ and $^i$ collapse onto SplitNode.copy0 and SplitNode.copy1 respectively, and $J \cup (V \sm W)$ collapses onto SplitNode.unsplit via the LN's shorthand convention. (Identifying $^o$ with copy0 and $^i$ with copy1 matches the convention used in def_3_11/claim_3_7 in this subsection.)

Well-typedness of the iterated SWIG. The inner two terms of the displayed equality below are iterated SWIGs of the form $\lp G_{\swig(W_i)} \rp_{\swig(W_j)}$ (with $\{i, j\} = \{1, 2\}$), in which $\swig(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\swig(W_i)}$ produced by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention. For this outer application to be well-typed per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's precondition “$W \ins V$ of the input CDMG”, we need $W_j$ to be (a subset of) the output-node set of $G_{\swig(W_i)}$, i.e.\ \[ W_j \;\ins\; V_{\swig(W_i)} \;=\; (V \sm W_i) \;\dcup\; W_i^o, \] where the equality of carriers is item ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention and $W_i^o := \lC w^o \st w \in W_i \rC$ is the output-side tagged copy of $W_i$ introduced there (note that, in contrast to regular node-splitting, the input-side copy $W_i^i$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_i)}$ rather than $V_{\swig(W_i)}$, so the iterated output-node set carries only the single tagged copy $W_i^o$). By the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, every $w \in W_j$ satisfies $w \in V$ (since $W_j \ins V$) and $w \notin W_i$ (by disjointness), so $w \in V \sm W_i$. Through def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's notational shorthand “$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_i)$”, the set $V \sm W_i$ is realised as the untagged piece of the disjoint union $V_{\swig(W_i)} = (V \sm W_i) \dcup W_i^o$, i.e.\ the inclusion \[ V \sm W_i \;\hookrightarrow\; V_{\swig(W_i)} \] sends $v \mapsto v$ unchanged. Hence \[ W_j \;\ins\; V \sm W_i \;\ins\; V_{\swig(W_i)}, \] so $\lp G_{\swig(W_i)} \rp_{\swig(W_j)}$ is defined per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention. The analogous spell-out for the swapped pair (with $i$ and $j$ exchanged) is the same argument with the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ exchanged, using the same disjointness condition $W_1 \cap W_2 = W_2 \cap W_1 = \emptyset$. The right-hand-side single SWIG $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ is well-typed per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention because $W_1 \dcup W_2 \ins V$: by $W_1 \ins V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ together with disjointness, the union $W_1 \cup W_2$ is itself a disjoint union $W_1 \dcup W_2$ and remains a subset of $V$.

The conclusion. The CDMGs $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$, $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$, and $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def def:G_node-splitting_intervention as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \] is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:

  1. Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$. \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]
  2. Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \]

Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's “symmetry under swap” reading \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \] by transitivity of equality on the shared right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

The equalities in (a) and (b) are equalities of CDMGs (def def-cdmg) read up to the canonical bijection of the iterated and single-SWIG node-set carriers induced by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's unsplit-injection convention “$v^o := v^i := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$” (the iterated carriers $J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$, $V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$ and the single-step carriers $J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, $V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ are not literally the same sets as produced by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, but the corresponding pairs are canonically identified by matching corresponding tagged copies: under the Lean SplitNode encoding this identification is exactly the flattenSplit map introduced in claim_3_7, which collapses SplitNode (SplitNode Node) onto SplitNode Node by sending nested copy0/copy1/unsplit constructors to the appropriate single-level constructor, and the present row's Lean file is expected to import that map verbatim from claim_3_7's solved file).

Proof.

We prove sub-claim (a), $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, by checking the equality of the two CDMGs componentwise on each of the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg in turn; sub-claim (b), $\lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, is then obtained by an explicit symmetry argument at the end. Both sub-claims rely on the well-typedness check already established in the statement block above to license the outer $\swig(\cdot)$ application: in particular, by the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, every $w \in W_2$ satisfies $w \in V \sm W_1 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ (and symmetrically every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in V \sm W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_2)}$).

Notation for SWIG copies. For $k \in \{1, 2\}$ and $w \in W_k$, write $w^{o_k}$ and $w^{i_k}$ for the two tagged copies produced by $\swig(W_k)$ per def def:G_node-splitting_intervention; the subscript $k$ records which of the two SWIG steps introduced the tag. The output-side copy $w^{o_k}$ sits in $V_{\swig(W_k)}$ while the input-side copy $w^{i_k}$ sits in $J_{\swig(W_k)}$ (items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention), in contrast to regular node-splitting (def def:G_node-splitting) where both tagged copies sit in the output-node set. By def def:G_node-splitting_intervention's unsplit-injection shorthand, $v^{o_k} := v^{i_k} := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W_k)$. For an element $v \in J \cup V$ we will also write $v^i$ and $v^o$ for the single-step tagging used on $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$:

  • $v^i := v^{i_1}$ and $v^o := v^{o_1}$ if $v \in W_1$,
  • $v^i := v^{i_2}$ and $v^o := v^{o_2}$ if $v \in W_2$,
  • $v^i := v^o := v$ if $v \in J \cup (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2))$

(the three cases are exhaustive and pairwise disjoint by disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$; the $o$-version is only used for $v \in V$ since $L \ins V \times V$ and the output-side tagging is never applied to inputs $v \in J$ in $E$ or $L$). This is the canonical bijection of carriers spelled out at the bottom of the statement block.

Proof of (a): composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$. We verify the equality $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ on each of the four components.

Input nodes ($J$-component). By item i.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the inner SWIG, \[ J_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^{i_1}. \] Applying item i.\ again to the outer SWIG (acting on the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, with split set $W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ as licensed by the well-typedness check), \[ J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{\swig(W_1)} \;\dcup\; W_2^{i_2} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^{i_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{i_2}. \] On the other hand, item i.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the single-step SWIG on $G$ with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2$ gives \[ J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; (W_1 \dcup W_2)^i, \] and the tag-by-tag identification of the single-step input-side copies $(W_1 \dcup W_2)^i = W_1^{i_1} \dcup W_2^{i_2}$ (per the canonical bijection of carriers in the notation block above) yields \[ J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; J \;\dcup\; W_1^{i_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{i_2}. \] The two input-node sets match. ✓

Output nodes ($V$-component). By item ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the outer SWIG (acting on the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, with split set $W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$), \[ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lp V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2 \rp \;\dcup\; W_2^{o_2}. \] Item ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the inner SWIG gives $V_{\swig(W_1)} = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1}$. By disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, the elements of $W_2 \ins V$ all lie in $V \sm W_1$, and (via the unsplit-injection identification $V \sm W_1 \hookrightarrow V_{\swig(W_1)}$) they embed into the untagged piece of the above disjoint union, disjointly from $W_1^{o_1}$. Removing $W_2$ from $V_{\swig(W_1)}$ thus only affects the untagged piece, giving \[ V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2 \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_1^{o_1}. \] Substituting, \[ V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_1^{o_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{o_2}. \] On the other hand, item ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the single-step SWIG on $G$ with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2$ gives \[ V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)) \;\dcup\; (W_1 \dcup W_2)^o, \] and $V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2) = V \sm W_1 \sm W_2$ together with the tag-by-tag identification $(W_1 \dcup W_2)^o = W_1^{o_1} \dcup W_2^{o_2}$ (per the canonical bijection of carriers) yields \[ V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \;\dcup\; W_1^{o_1} \;\dcup\; W_2^{o_2}. \] The two output-node sets match. ✓

Directed edges ($E$-component). By item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the inner SWIG, \[ E_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; \lC v_1^{i_1} \tuh v_2^{o_1} \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC. \] (Note that this is a single set-comprehension; there is no separate transfer-edge contribution as there is for regular node-splitting in def def:G_node-splitting, since item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention omits the “$\dcup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \st w \in W \rC$” clause – the SWIG construction does not introduce inner transfer arcs from $W^o$ back to $W^i$.) Applying the outer $\swig(W_2)$ to the input CDMG $G_{\swig(W_1)}$, item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention gives \[ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1')^{i_2} \tuh (v_2')^{o_2} \st v_1' \tuh v_2' \in E_{\swig(W_1)} \rC. \] We trace each generator $v_1^{i_1} \tuh v_2^{o_1}$ of $E_{\swig(W_1)}$ (arising from $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E$, so $v_1 \in J \cup V$ and $v_2 \in V$ by def def-cdmg) through the outer tagging, case-splitting on each endpoint independently.

Head endpoint $v_1^{i_1}$. The three cases are exhaustive and pairwise disjoint by $J \cap V = \emptyset$ together with the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$:

  • Case $v_1 \in W_1$. Then $v_1^{i_1} \in W_1^{i_1}$ by the inner-SWIG tagged copy. By item i.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the inner SWIG, $W_1^{i_1} \ins J_{\swig(W_1)}$, hence by def def-cdmg's axiom $J_{\swig(W_1)} \cap V_{\swig(W_1)} = \emptyset$ on the inner CDMG, the tagged piece $W_1^{i_1}$ is disjoint from the outer SWIG's input subset $W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$; in particular $v_1^{i_1} \notin W_2$. The outer unsplit-injection $u^{i_2} := u$ for $u \in J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup (V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2)$ then gives $(v_1^{i_1})^{i_2} = v_1^{i_1} = v_1^i$ (in the $v^i$ notation of the notation block, using the case “$v_1 \in W_1$”).
  • Case $v_1 \in W_2$. Then $v_1 \in V$ (since $W_2 \ins V$), and by disjointness $v_1 \notin W_1$, so the inner unsplit-injection gives $v_1^{i_1} = v_1$. The outer SWIG then maps $v_1 \mapsto v_1^{i_2} = v_1^i$ (using the case “$v_1 \in W_2$” of the notation block).
  • Case $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2))$. Then $v_1 \notin W_1$ and $v_1 \notin W_2$, so $v_1^{i_1} = v_1$ (inner unsplit). The outer SWIG then fixes $v_1$: since $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)) \ins J \cup (V \sm W_2) \ins J_{\swig(W_1)} \cup (V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2)$ (the second inclusion uses the inner SWIG's $J \dcup W_1^{i_1} = J_{\swig(W_1)}$ and $(V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1} = V_{\swig(W_1)}$, into which $J$ embeds and $V \sm W_1 \sm W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$ embeds via the unsplit-injection), we have $(v_1)^{i_2} = v_1 = v_1^i$ (using the third case of the notation block).

Tail endpoint $v_2^{o_1}$. The same three-case analysis, with $o_k$ in place of $i_k$ throughout and the slightly tighter typing $v_2 \in V$ (so the third case reads “$v_2 \in V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)$”):

  • Case $v_2 \in W_1$. Then $v_2^{o_1} \in W_1^{o_1}$ by the inner-SWIG tagged copy. By item ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, $W_1^{o_1} \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ and is disjoint from the untagged piece $V \sm W_1 \supseteq W_2$ in the disjoint union $V_{\swig(W_1)} = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1}$; hence $v_2^{o_1} \notin W_2$. The outer unsplit-injection then gives $(v_2^{o_1})^{o_2} = v_2^{o_1} = v_2^o$ (using the case “$v_2 \in W_1$” of the notation block).
  • Case $v_2 \in W_2$. Then by disjointness $v_2 \notin W_1$, so the inner unsplit-injection gives $v_2^{o_1} = v_2$. The outer SWIG then maps $v_2 \mapsto v_2^{o_2} = v_2^o$ (using the case “$v_2 \in W_2$” of the notation block).
  • Case $v_2 \in V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)$. Then $v_2 \notin W_1$ and $v_2 \notin W_2$, so $v_2^{o_1} = v_2$ (inner unsplit) and $v_2 \in V \sm W_1 \sm W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_1)} \sm W_2$, so $(v_2)^{o_2} = v_2 = v_2^o$ (outer unsplit, using the third case of the notation block).

Hence the image of $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E$ under the iterated tagging is exactly $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o$, and the collection of such images is \[ \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC. \] On the other hand, item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the single-step SWIG with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2 \ins V$ yields directly \[ E_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \] under the same $v^i$/$v^o$ tagging conventions. Hence \[ E_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; E_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \] ✓

Bidirected edges ($L$-component). By item iv.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the inner SWIG, \[ L_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; \lC v_1^{o_1} \huh v_2^{o_1} \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \] Applying the outer $\swig(W_2)$, item iv.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention gives \[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC (v_1')^{o_2} \huh (v_2')^{o_2} \st v_1' \huh v_2' \in L_{\swig(W_1)} \rC. \] For a generator $v_1^{o_1} \huh v_2^{o_1}$ (arising from $v_1 \huh v_2 \in L$, with $v_1, v_2 \in V$ by def def-cdmg's typing $L \ins V \times V$), the same three-case analysis as for the tail endpoint of the directed-edge case applies to each $o$-superscripted endpoint independently:

  • Case $v \in W_1$. Then $v^{o_1} \in W_1^{o_1} \ins V_{\swig(W_1)}$ and (by the inner-SWIG carrier identity $V_{\swig(W_1)} = (V \sm W_1) \dcup W_1^{o_1}$ together with disjointness $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ giving $W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$) $v^{o_1} \notin W_2$; the outer unsplit-injection fixes it: $(v^{o_1})^{o_2} = v^{o_1} = v^o$.
  • Case $v \in W_2$. Then by disjointness $v \notin W_1$, so the inner unsplit-injection gives $v^{o_1} = v$; the outer SWIG then maps $v \mapsto v^{o_2} = v^o$.
  • Case $v \in V \sm (W_1 \dcup W_2)$. Then $v \notin W_1$ and $v \notin W_2$, so $v^{o_1} = v$ (inner unsplit) and $(v)^{o_2} = v = v^o$ (outer unsplit). (The fourth a priori case “$v \in J$” is excluded by def def-cdmg's constraint $L \ins V \times V$, which forces both endpoints of any $L$-generator to lie in $V$; so the three cases above are exhaustive on the endpoints actually appearing in the set-builder for $L$.)

Hence \[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \] On the other hand, item iv.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention applied to the single-step SWIG with split set $W_1 \dcup W_2$ yields exactly the same set under the same $v^o$ tagging: \[ L_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC. \] Hence \[ L_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}} \;=\; L_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}. \] ✓

Combining the four componentwise equalities above, the CDMGs $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}$ and $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ agree field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def def-cdmg). This establishes sub-claim (a).

Proof of (b): composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$. Sub-claim (b) is obtained by swapping the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the argument for (a). The four-component verification above depends on the pair of subsets only through their symbolic roles: the input-node identity and the output-node identity follow from items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting_intervention; the directed-edge and bidirected-edge identities follow from items iii.\ and iv., respectively, together with the disjointness hypothesis $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, which is symmetric in $(W_1, W_2)$ ($W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset \Leftrightarrow W_2 \cap W_1 = \emptyset$). The well-typedness side-condition needed to license the outer $\swig(\cdot)$ application also has a symmetric counterpart, established in the statement block: every $w \in W_1$ satisfies $w \in V \sm W_2 \ins V_{\swig(W_2)}$. Re-running the four-component check above with the substitution $W_1 \leftrightarrow W_2$ throughout therefore yields \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_2 \dcup W_1)}. \] This is precisely sub-claim (a) instantiated at the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$. We emphasise that sub-claim (b) is not a literal instance of (a): the iterated SWIG on the left-hand side of (b) is built from the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$, and the right-hand-side disjoint union appearing in (a) for that swapped pair is $W_2 \dcup W_1$, not the $W_1 \dcup W_2$ in the statement of (b). The two right-hand sides agree by commutativity of disjoint union, $W_2 \dcup W_1 = W_1 \dcup W_2$, which gives \[ G_{\swig(W_2 \dcup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \] as CDMGs: def def:G_node-splitting_intervention depends on $W$ only through the underlying set $W \ins V$ (each of items i.–iv.\ refers to $W$ only via the operations $V \sm W$, $W^o$, $W^i$, and the membership predicate “$\cdot \in W$”), and equal sets produce equal CDMGs componentwise. Chaining the two equalities, \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_2 \dcup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}, \] establishes sub-claim (b).

Recovery of the LN's triple equality. By sub-claims (a) and (b) – both of which equate their respective iterated SWIG with the single right-hand side $G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ – transitivity of equality on that shared right-hand side yields \[ \lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}, \] which is the LN's stated triple equality and in particular recovers the swap-symmetry reading $\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)} = \lp G_{\swig(W_2)} \rp_{\swig(W_1)}$.

Carrier-bijection convention. The equalities above are read up to the canonical bijection of iterated and single-step SWIG node-set carriers spelled out at the bottom of the statement block: the iterated carriers $J_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$, $V_{\lp G_{\swig(W_1)} \rp_{\swig(W_2)}}$ and their single-step counterparts $J_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$, $V_{\swig(W_1 \dcup W_2)}$ are not literally the same sets as produced by def def:G_node-splitting_intervention, but the corresponding pairs are canonically identified by matching tagged copies. Under the Lean SplitNode encoding, this identification is exactly the flattenSplit map introduced in claim_3_7 (which collapses SplitNode (SplitNode Node) onto SplitNode Node by sending nested copy0/copy1/unsplit constructors to the appropriate single-level constructor, and is imported verbatim by the present row's Lean file); the equalities “$=$” in sub-claims (a) and (b) above and in the LN's displayed triple equality are read along that map.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V` and on `twoDisjointNodeSplittingHardCommute`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the statement constructs `W₁ ∪ W₂` (`Finset.union`), `W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit` (`Finset.image`), the iterated SWIG carriers that unfold to `G.V \ W` (`Finset.sdiff`), and the four field-wise `Finset.image flattenSplit` equalities packaged inside `eqViaNodeMap` — each of which needs decidable equality on `Node` (and, via the auto-derived `DecidableEq (SplitNode Node)` and `DecidableEq (SplitNode (SplitNode Node))` instances inherited from `def_3_11`'s `SplitNode` `inductive`, on the iterated and single-step carriers as well). Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.

lean_blocks.explanation: This private helper discharges the inner-`hW` precondition of the *outer* `nodeSplittingHard` call in the iterated SWIG `(G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingHard ?_ (W₂.image .unsplit) ?_`. The outer constructor needs `W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit ⊆ (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).V`, and `def_3_12`'s $V_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1)}$ unfolds to `(G.V ∖ W₁).image .unsplit ∪ W₁.image .copy0` — so an element of `W₂.image .unsplit` must land in the `.unsplit`-tagged piece. Disjointness `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is the load-bearing hypothesis: any `v ∈ W₂` lies outside `W₁`, hence inside `G.V ∖ W₁`, hence its `.unsplit` image sits in `(G.V ∖ W₁).image .unsplit`. This is the *two-piece-union* analogue of `claim_3_7`'s `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingOn_V`: the `.copy1` summand present in the regular-spl carrier is missing here because `def_3_12` reclassifies the input-side tagged copies to $J_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1)}$. The helper is symmetric in `W₁` / `W₂` and is reused for both directions (a) and (b) of the main theorem via `hDisj` / `hDisj.symm`. `Finset.disjoint_right.mp` and `Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr` are the Mathlib idioms that drive the proof.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Private helper: discharges the inner-`hW` precondition of the outer SWIG; two-piece-union analogue of `claim_3_7`'s spl helper.
private lemma image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V
    -- Implicit `G` and `hG`; `hG` is referenced inside the conclusion via the inner `nodeSplittingHard` application.
    {G : CDMG Node} {hG : G.IsCADMG}
    -- `W₁` (inner SWIG's split set) and `W₂` (outer SWIG's split set) as implicit `Finset Node`s.
    {W₁ W₂ : Finset Node}
    -- Explicit subset hypotheses for both `W₁`, `W₂`; `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is `def_3_12`'s LN-faithful rendering of `W₁ ∩ W₂ = ∅`.
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- Conclusion: `W₂` lifted by `.unsplit` lands in the inner SWIG's output-node set, licensing the outer `nodeSplittingHard`.
    W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit ⊆
      (G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).V

lean_blocks.code:
theorem twoDisjointNodeSplittingHardCommute (G : CDMG Node)
    (hG : G.IsCADMG) (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node)
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    eqViaNodeMap
        ((G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingHard
            (swigAcyclic G hG W₁ hW₁)
            (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit)
            (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V hW₁ hW₂ hDisj))
        (G.nodeSplittingHard hG (W₁ ∪ W₂)
            (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂))
        flattenSplit
      ∧
    eqViaNodeMap
        ((G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingHard
            (swigAcyclic G hG W₂ hW₂)
            (W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit)
            (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V hW₂ hW₁ hDisj.symm))
        (G.nodeSplittingHard hG (W₁ ∪ W₂)
            (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂))
        flattenSplit
lean_blocks.explanation: This is the row's headline declaration: the SWIG analogue of `claim_3_7`'s `twoDisjointNodeSplittingCommute`. It asserts that for any CADMG `G` and any two disjoint `W₁`, `W₂ ⊆ G.V`, both iteration orders of `nodeSplittingHard` agree (up to `flattenSplit`) with the single-step joint intervention `G.nodeSplittingHard hG (W₁ ∪ W₂) _`. The conclusion is a conjunction `⟨(a), (b)⟩` of two `eqViaNodeMap` equalities through the shared right-hand side; the LN's triple equality $(G_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1)})_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)} = (G_{\mathrm{swig}(W_2)})_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1)} = G_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is recovered by transitivity on this shared RHS. The outer `nodeSplittingHard` in each iterated form consumes two non-trivial witnesses: `swigAcyclic G hG Wᵢ hWᵢ` (from `claim_3_9`) for the inner-`hG`, and the local helper `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V` for the inner-`hW`. The single helper covers both directions via `hDisj` and `hDisj.symm`. The carrier-mismatch between iterated `SplitNode (SplitNode Node)` and single-step `SplitNode Node` is bridged by `flattenSplit`, imported verbatim from `claim_3_7`'s solved file along with `eqViaNodeMap`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Main theorem: iterated SWIG `(G_{swig(W₁)})_{swig(W₂)}` agrees with the joint `G_{swig(W₁ ∪ W₂)}` via `flattenSplit`, both orders.
theorem twoDisjointNodeSplittingHardCommute (G : CDMG Node)
    -- `hG` is the acyclicity witness; `def_3_12`'s `nodeSplittingHard` consumes it on every SWIG application.
    (hG : G.IsCADMG) (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node)
    -- Subset hypotheses `W₁, W₂ ⊆ G.V`; `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is Mathlib's `Finset.Disjoint`, the LN's `W₁ ∩ W₂ = ∅`.
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- Sub-claim (a): `(G_{swig(W₁)})_{swig(W₂)} = G_{swig(W₁ ∪ W₂)}` read up to `flattenSplit`.
    eqViaNodeMap
        ((G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingHard
            -- Inner-`hG`: acyclicity of the inner SWIG, supplied by `claim_3_9.swigAcyclic`.
            (swigAcyclic G hG W₁ hW₁)
            -- Outer split set: `W₂` lifted along `.unsplit` to live in the inner SWIG's carrier.
            (W₂.image SplitNode.unsplit)
            -- Inner-`hW`: discharged by the local two-piece-union helper above.
            (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V hW₁ hW₂ hDisj))
        -- RHS: single-step joint SWIG on `W₁ ∪ W₂`; `Finset.union_subset` combines the two subset hypotheses.
        (G.nodeSplittingHard hG (W₁ ∪ W₂)
            (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂))
        -- Node map: imported from `claim_3_7`'s file; collapses `SplitNode (SplitNode Node)` to `SplitNode Node`.
        flattenSplit
      ∧
    -- Sub-claim (b): swapped iteration order `(G_{swig(W₂)})_{swig(W₁)} = G_{swig(W₁ ∪ W₂)}` via the same `flattenSplit`.
    eqViaNodeMap
        ((G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₂ hW₂).nodeSplittingHard
            -- Inner-`hG` for the swapped order: `swigAcyclic` on `W₂` instead of `W₁`.
            (swigAcyclic G hG W₂ hW₂)
            -- Outer split set: `W₁` lifted along `.unsplit`.
            (W₁.image SplitNode.unsplit)
            -- Inner-`hW`: same helper with arguments swapped; `hDisj.symm` flips `Disjoint W₁ W₂` to `Disjoint W₂ W₁`.
            (image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V hW₂ hW₁ hDisj.symm))
        -- Same RHS as (a): the joint SWIG on `W₁ ∪ W₂` is independent of iteration order at the `Finset.union` level.
        (G.nodeSplittingHard hG (W₁ ∪ W₂)
            (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂))
        flattenSplit

design_choices:
**Decompose the LN's triple equality into a conjunction `(a) ∧ (b)` with the joint intervention $G_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ as the shared right-hand side.** Lean has no native triple equality. Both iteration orders are rendered against the single-step joint intervention as their common reference, and the LN's swap-symmetry reading is recovered as the transitive composite. Same conjunction shape as `claim_3_4` (`HardInterventionsCommute`) and the regular-spl sibling `claim_3_7`; reusing it keeps all three "commute" rows callable at parallel `.1` / `.2` projections.

**Use `eqViaNodeMap iter single flattenSplit`, not a direct equality between the two iterated forms.** Both `iter₁₂` and `iter₂₁` inhabit `CDMG (SplitNode (SplitNode Node))`, but a node $w_1 \in W_1$ wears different constructor wrappings on each side (e.g. `.unsplit (.copy0 w₁)` versus `.copy0 (.unsplit w₁)` in the `V` field). A literal `iter₁₂ = iter₂₁` is *false* as a Lean proposition. Routing both sides through the canonical single-step graph via the *same* `flattenSplit` relabelling is the strongest equality available without introducing quotient types or a `CDMG.Iso` layer. `flattenSplit` and `eqViaNodeMap` are imported verbatim from `claim_3_7`'s file — both are pure constructor-algebra, and the inhabiting patterns under SWIG are a subset of those under spl, so one flatten map handles both.

**Encode the disjoint union as `W₁ ∪ W₂` paired with `Disjoint W₁ W₂`, not as a `Sum`-typed `⊔`.** Matches `def_3_12`'s `nodeSplittingHard` API (`W : Finset Node`, `hW : W ⊆ G.V`); the right-hand side is `G.nodeSplittingHard hG (W₁ ∪ W₂) (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂)`. `Finset.Disjoint` reads the LN's $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ directly and plays cleanly with `Finset.union_subset`, with no `Finset.disjUnion` coercion gymnastics. Matches `claim_3_7`'s choice exactly.

**Keep the CDMG structure unbundled, with `hG : G.IsCADMG` as a separate propositional argument.** Following `def_3_7`'s convention, CADMG is the predicate `IsCADMG` (definitionally `IsAcyclic`) on top of `CDMG`, not a subtype. Bundling would force a `.val`-projection at every reference to $G$; the unbundled shape keeps the asserted equality between two CDMGs at exactly the form the LN writes.

**Feed `swigAcyclic` (from `claim_3_9`) into the outer iterated SWIG's inner-`hG` slot.** The iterated form `(G.nodeSplittingHard hG W₁ hW₁).nodeSplittingHard ?_ …` needs an `IsCADMG` witness on the inner SWIG-output graph — non-trivial, and precisely the content of `claim_3_9`. Without that prior result the theorem would not type-check; `claim_3_9` was ordered before this row specifically to discharge the dependency. The same plumbing pattern is available to any later row needing nested SWIGs.

**The helper `image_unsplit_subset_nodeSplittingHard_V` is the two-piece-union analogue of `claim_3_7`'s three-piece-union helper.** Under SWIG, $V_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1)}$ contains only the output-side tagged copies `W₁.image .copy0` together with the untagged piece — the input-side copies `W₁.image .copy1` have moved to $J_{\mathrm{swig}(W_1)}$. The membership chain therefore takes one `Finset.mem_union_left` fewer than the regular-splitting analogue. A single helper covers both directions (a) and (b) by symmetry: instantiated as `(hW₁, hW₂, hDisj)` for (a) and `(hW₂, hW₁, hDisj.symm)` for (b). Marked `private` to localise it to this file, mirroring `claim_3_4` and `claim_3_7`.
